best_image_source: Esri World Imagery aerial photo dated 11/13/2025
roof_system: Mixed roof types: TPO, PVC or Coated Roof

possible_roof_systems.evidence: Large central roof field is smooth, light-colored, and membrane-like with faint linear seam/lap indications; the aerial view supports a white single-ply roof but does not resolve details to separate TPO from PVC or coating.

The smaller attached bright white roof section has a uniform monolithic look with localized discoloration/repair-like dark spots; a PVC membrane or coated roof is plausible, but the image does not separate the two.
Some faint darker linear marks and weathering artifacts could be read as another membrane family, but the roof does not show the broad dark matte field needed to favor a dark single-ply system.

roof_age_estimate: Likely mid-life to older low-slope roof based on discoloration, faint seam visibility, and localized weathering; exact age unknown from aerial imagery.

notes: Localized dark stains/discoloration are visible on the bright white attached roof section.
Uneven roof color and faint linear marks suggest weathering and possible prior repairs or drainage-related staining.
Several rooftop penetrations/equipment curbs are visible across the roof, increasing flashing and leak risk on a low-slope assembly.
No clear overhanging trees or roof debris are visible from this aerial view.

observations: Visible risk factors include dark staining or inconsistent roof color, possible ponding or drainage stress, high penetration or rooftop-unit density.
Main central roof field is a large smooth light membrane-like area with faint linear seam/lap patterns.
Right/east attached roof section is brighter white and visually distinct from the main field.
Localized dark blotches/discoloration are visible on the attached white section.
Multiple rooftop units, vents, and curbs are present, creating numerous flashing locations.
summary: Esri World Imagery dated 11/13/2025 shows a mixed low-slope commercial roof at 6345 Colorado Blvd, Commerce City, CO. The dominant field appears to be a smooth light single-ply membrane, best fit TPO from aerial evidence, while the attached bright white section could be TPO, PVC, or a coated roof. Visible risk factors include dark staining or inconsistent roof color, possible ponding or drainage stress, high penetration or rooftop-unit density.
recommendation: Have a qualified commercial roof-coating contractor or membrane roofing contractor perform an onsite inspection, moisture testing where warranted, and confirmation of the roof system, flashing details, repairs, and warranty eligibility, then provide a reliable project-price proposal. Because the roof appears to be a restorable low-slope membrane system rather than asphalt shingles, evaluate whether a silicone roof restoration is feasible before considering overlay or replacement; a qualifying silicone system may reduce tear-off waste, cost, and disruption, create a seamless monolithic white reflective cool-roof surface, withstand ponding water when properly designed and installed, improve resistance to UV exposure and hail, and may qualify for a manufacturer warranty of up to 20 years. If onsite inspection finds widespread moisture, failed insulation, deck/structural deterioration, or an incompatible substrate, prioritize overlay or re,

supporting_cues: Uniform membrane-like appearance across the dominant roof area
limitations: Aerial resolution is too soft to resolve weld seams, lap widths, or coating/application patterns.
Color and smoothness alone cannot confirm exact membrane chemistry.

supporting_cues: Smaller attached bright white roof field
Localized dark staining/discoloration and repair-like mottling
limitations: The image does not resolve whether the finish is PVC membrane or a coating over another substrate.
Edge and flashing details are visible only in soft aerial detail.

candidates.evidence: Large smooth light/white low-slope field with broad membrane-like appearance; faint linear marks suggest sheet laps or weathering, but the image is too soft to resolve seam details needed to separate TPO from PVC or coating.
Same light uniform roof field could be PVC membrane, but there are no clear markings, seam characteristics, or edge details to confirm it from this aerial image.

A reflective or coated surface is possible because the roof reads as a light monolithic field, but the imagery does not show application pattern, patching, or substrate telegraphing clearly enough to support it confidently.

limitations: Image resolution and sharpness are insufficient to resolve weld seams, lap width, or surface application pattern.
Color alone is not enough to distinguish TPO, PVC, and coating; confidence is capped accordingly.

candidates.evidence: Attached bright white smooth low-slope zone with a membrane-like finish; however, seams and surface detail are not resolved well enough to distinguish TPO from PVC or coating.
Uniform white appearance and smooth field could fit PVC, but the aerial lacks the close detail needed for a firmer call.
A coated roof is possible, especially given the bright monolithic look, but there is no clear evidence of coating texture, wear-through, or irregular application pattern.

limitations: The zone is visually distinct from the main roof but the imagery still does not resolve the cues needed for exact membrane chemistry.
Visible roof-edge details and seams are too soft to raise confidence above the conservative cap.

overall_limitations: Aerial image sharpness/resolution is limited for material discrimination; seams, ribs, and application patterns are not clearly resolved.
The roof appears to have at least two materially different low-slope zones, but both are interpreted conservatively due to insufficient close detail.
Overall confidence is capped at 60 because the leading type rests on unresolved details in poor imagery.
